text: An API, or Application Programming Interface, allows different software applications to communicate with each other. When working with APIs, you typically make HTTP requests to a server. The most common types are GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE, depending on what you want to do. Most modern APIs return data in JSON format, which is easy for computers to read and process. Many APIs require authentication, often using an API key or token, to control access to their data. Additionally, you should be aware of rate limits, which restrict how many requests you can make within a certain time. Finally, proper error handling is essential. Always check the response status codes and handle errors like timeouts or invalid data gracefully in your code.
